results.coaching_summary: This was a clean, low-complexity HCP drug-interaction inquiry with no genuine AE, PC, or special situation per ground truth. The trainee handled the core inquiry well: correct product selection, correct SRL citation (SRL-CDZ-INR), accurate on-label interaction answer, complete HCP contact capture, and a proper courteous close. However, after the call was already wrapped up appropriately, the trainee re-engaged the caller with an unsolicited, fishing-style question ('have you noticed any headaches or other side effects at all, even minor ones?'). The caller had not volunteered any symptom cue, and even after being probed, explicitly said she wasn't calling about that — this is a classic manufactured-AE scenario the platform is designed to catch. The trainee then compounded the error by documenting this fabricated signal as an actual AE (ae_present=yes) without following through on any of the required AE workflow (no routing, no timeframe, no proper description, inconsistent resolution/closure). This creates real regulatory risk in a live environment: a phantom AE case would either sit unrouted (a compliance gap) or trigger unnecessary PV workflow based on manufactured data, both of which erode data integrity and reporting timeliness metrics.

results.coaching_summary: Strong verbal performance undermined by critical documentation failure. AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S5.2): On the call, Alex did exactly the right thing — caught Pat Morgan's volunteered 'shaky' cue, paused the flow, and asked clarifying questions that surfaced a clear, four-element AE (tremor + palpitations, ~1 week onset, ongoing, identifiable patient/reporter/product). This is textbook listen-and-clarify technique (Questioning Technique/S1.3 rated 4 — exceeds). However, the documentation record completely contradicts the call: Safety tab shows ae_present='no' with all four elements marked false and an empty ae_description, despite routing_single listing PV. This is the single most consequential gap in the case — a real AE was verbally captured but never actually documented, meaning it would never reach pharmacovigilance for real safety follow-up. In a live QA environment, this discrepancy would trigger a critical safety compliance finding: the case would be flagged as a missed/under-reported AE regardless of what was said on the phone, since regulatory reporting depends on the written record, not the transcript. Empathy (S1.2) and Compliance (S5.1/S5.3) were both handled well — no medical advice given, appropriate HCP referral, non-promotional tone. Constructive feedback: whenever an AE is clarified and confirmed verbally, the ae_present flag, four-element checklist, and ae_description/onset fields must be populated to match — treat the Safety tab as the actual deliverable, not the call script. Recommend a documentation-consistency check before closing any case where a safety signal was verbally confirmed.

results.coaching_summary: Strong overall handling of a complex dual PC+AE case. AE/PC Detection: Both the product complaint (cloudy solution, bent needle) and the adverse event (perioral tingling/numbness, possible hypocalcemia) were correctly identified from caller-volunteered cues and routed to both PV and Quality — no fabrication, no missed signals. Questioning Technique: Excellent listen-and-clarify approach; the trainee explicitly flagged the volunteered cues and asked clarifying follow-ups rather than fishing, exactly per SOP. Compliance: No off-label material was introduced, and the trainee appropriately restricted comments to the approved SRL, referring the caller to their HCP for urgent evaluation of hypocalcemia symptoms — good regulatory hygiene. Documentation: This is the area needing coaching. While all core safety and PC identifier fields (lot, NDC, expiry, availability) were properly captured, the resolution/completion narrative for both the AE and PC was left essentially blank — only checklist booleans were toggled with no substantive notes on next steps or case resolution. In a live QA audit this would trigger a documentation completeness finding, since reviewers need a clear textual record of what action was taken/pending for each reported issue, not just checkboxes.

results.coaching_summary:
This SC-04 case required detecting BOTH a product complaint (cloudy solution, bent needle) and an adverse event (perioral tingling/numbness, hypocalcemia) with dual routing to PV and Quality. AE/PC Detection: The PC was well identified and documented (S3.1, S3.4, S3.6, S3.7 all pass), but the AE was completely mishandled — the documentation explicitly marks ae_present as 'no' and leaves the AE description, onset, and four-element test blank, directly contradicting the ground truth that an AE was present and required. Questioning Technique: The transcript shows the trainee jumping from 'I want a replacement' straight to reading an SRL about tingling/numbness the caller never actually described on the call — the two key volunteered cues in the reveal rules (the PC details and the 'felt a bit off since' AE cue) were never surfaced or clarified in the visible dialogue, so the SRL selection and symptom description appear to have been assumed rather than obtained through active listening. Documentation/Regulatory risk: Because the AE was marked absent, dual routing to PV never occurred (route_to_pv: false) and even the Quality routing for the PC was left false — meaning neither report was actually routed anywhere, despite the case explicitly requiring dual routing. This is a critical regulatory gap: an unrouted AE with hypocalcemia symptoms and an unrouted product complaint with a bent needle both represent real patient safety and quality risks that would trigger significant compliance findings in a live QA audit. Compliance/Empathy: tone was professional and the HCP referral language was appropriate and non-promotional. 

Coaching focus: (1) Always let the caller state their concern in their own words before reading any SRL — do not introduce specific symptom details the caller hasn't confirms. (2) When a caller mentions feeling 'off' or describes a product issue, ask a clarifying question to capture the full detail (onset, product identifiers) rather than moving straight to script. (3) Ensure ae_present and routing flags are set to match the actual clinical content of the call — here, the case type call for dual PV+Quality routing was entirely missed in the routing fields, which is the single highest-risk failure in this record.

results.coaching_summary:
This case involved a hostile caller with a legal/media threat alongside a serious, correctly identified AE (blistering rash, hospitalization, possible SJS). AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S5.2) was strong: the trainee caught the volunteered clinical cues and legal/media special situations, and routing (PV + legal + communications + supervisor) was documented correctly per the dual-routing requirement. Documentation of the AE description, contact info, SRL citation, and escalation routes was accurate and complete.

However, there is a critical Regulatory risk / Compliance failure: in response to caller pressure ("Can you at least say that?"), the trainee stated "I'm so sorry — the drug clearly caused this rash." This is a direct admission of causation/fault — exactly what SOP prohibits in AE/legal-threat scenarios — and it was carried into the documentation's verbal_answer_given field, compounding the risk by creating a written record of the admission. In a live QA environment, this statement alone would likely trigger a serious compliance escalation, as it could be used as an admission of liability in litigation and violates non-promotional, balanced-response requirements (S4.2, S4.5, S4.7, S5.3, S1.2).

Coaching focus: Reinforce de-escalation language that validates the caller's distress without confirming causation ("I understand how distressing this must be for you and your mother; I can't speculate on cause, but I want to make sure this is reported and reviewed appropriately"). Practice scripted non-committal empathy responses for hostile/legal-threat calls, and audit documentation to ensure no causation-admitting language is transcribed into the permanent record.

inquiry.summary:
A patient calls asking a simple logistics question: **how do I dispose of my leftover lower-dose Cardizan
tablets?** In passing they volunteer *why* they have leftovers — their doctor **increased the dose** because
the lower dose "wasn't getting my INR where it needed to be." That offhand line is a **lack-of-effect (LOE)**
cue: a subtherapeutic response on the starting dose. The teaching trap is that Cardizan's USPI *permits dose
titration*, so a trainee may rationalize the LOE away as "normal" and never capture it. Correct handling is to
**catch the cue, clarify, and capture the potential LOE for PV to assess** — flagging is not the same as
concluding the drug failed; that judgment belongs to PV — while answering the disposal question appropriately.

results.coaching_summary: Strong catch of the embedded lack-of-effect cue and correct PV routing/escalation, with clean disposal guidance and no medical advice given — good compliance instincts. However, a significant Questioning Technique/AE Detection error occurred: after the caller had already said goodbye and the case was fully resolved, the trainee reopened the call to solicit unrelated symptoms ('any headaches or other side effects at all, even minor ones?'). This is cold-canvassing, not listen-and-clarify, and it produced a fabricated AE (a 'mild headache... but I wasn't calling about that') that the trainee then documented as an adverse event in the Safety tab and AE Description field. In a live QA environment this would trigger a compliance/regulatory risk flag for over-reporting and could inflate PV caseloads with non-actionable signals, undermining trust in the AE intake process. Coach the trainee to close calls once the caller confirms nothing else is needed, and to only capture AEs/special situations that are volunteered or clarified from caller-raised cues — never solicited afterward.
